articles.title:
追蹤隈研吾十五年歷程的紀錄片《粒子的舞蹈》現正熱映中 | AXIS Web | 以設計觀點傳達人類可能性與創造力的媒體


Now I'm translating the author names and the opening paragraph, working through the key details about the documentary and its subject.

The film documents Kengo Kuma's architectural practice over 15 years, capturing his work across 16 countries and 80 projects—from post-earthquake reconstruction in Tohoku to the National Stadium and architectural education initiatives. I'm also noting the film's stylistic approach, using fragmented, particle-like imagery to capture moments of societal transformation, and highlighting how it portrays the recovery process in affected regions like Minamisanriku, Rikuzen Takada, and Tome.

The film serves as a documentary record of Japanese resilience and reconstruction wisdom. Director Hiromoto Oka describes it as an attempt to convey Kuma's sensitivity in connecting the intelligence of past, present, and future, and his ability to create tailored, place-based architecture through the accumulated fragments of his work.

The Image Forum screenings run through mid-April, and I'm considering catching it this weekend at the theater. The film is directed by Hiromoto Oka, who has spent 15 years following Kengo Kuma's architectural practice across 80 projects in 16 countries, documenting everything from post-earthquake reconstruction efforts to major stadiums and educational initiatives.

The work uses a pointillist approach through particle-like fragments to explore Kuma's questioning of architectural possibilities during times of social transformation like the Tokyo 2020 Games and the pandemic. It captures both the reconstruction happening in communities across Tohoku and the everyday life of those areas, creating a documentary record of Japan's approach to disaster recovery and the wisdom gained through that process.

Director Hiromoto Oka describes the film as a 15-year documentary following Kuma's architectural philosophy—connecting the intelligence of past, present, and future—through these fragmented, project-based moments, hoping viewers will absorb his place-based thinking and the insight that emerges from working with communities.
</think>
